---
title: "M05 Individual Reflection"
author: "Geoffrey Bruder"
date: "`r format(Sys.Date(), '%B %d, %Y')`"
output:
  pdf_document:
    latex_engine: xelatex
    toc: false
fontsize: 12pt
geometry: margin=1in
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE)
library(glue)
```

# M05 Individual Reflection

**Name:** Geoffrey Bruder  
**Group:** Zeta_WG_Repo — *County Childhood Vaccination Uptake*  
**Role (this week):** Lead / Primary analyst

## Reflection

This reflection summarizes my learning and contributions for M05. I focused on three themes: reproducibility and provenance, data quality and diagnostics, and interpretability for stakeholders.

**Reproducibility and provenance.** I implemented artifact capture (e.g., `outputs/run_provenance.json`) and ensured the pipeline writes canonical artifacts (`X_preprocessed.csv`, `y.csv`, `models_summary.json`). These artifacts make the analysis auditable and reproducible by other data scientists and external reviewers.

**Data quality and diagnostics.** The preprocessing audit revealed nontrivial missingness in survey-derived features (e.g., `vaccinate_children`) and several ACS variables that required group (state) median imputation. The imputation audit (kept_rows = 1543; dropped_rows = 45) and residual diagnostics (heavy tails, heteroskedasticity) indicate that model errors are driven partly by data gaps and partly by structural heterogeneity across counties.

**Interpretability and stakeholder translation.** ElasticNet was selected as the baseline because it balances predictive performance with coefficient-level interpretability. I prepared coefficient tables and SHAP export hooks to support per-county interpretability reports for public‑health stakeholders.

I explored these takeaways further and coordinated the nested CV runs and report knitting. Next steps I will prioritize: (1) implement split-conformal intervals that account for heteroskedasticity, (2) run temporal holdouts if time-indexed data are available, and (3) produce per-county one‑page summaries with SHAP explanations for the top counties identified as at risk.
